channel.title: NYT > Technology
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Elon Musk’s SpaceX Files to Go Public, Setting Stage for Huge I.P.O.
item.description: An initial public offering of Elon Musk’s rocket and satellite company would most likely be a generational wealth event and one of the largest offerings ever.
item.dc:creator: Ryan Mac, Lauren Hirsch and Maureen Farrell
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: SpaceX’s Starship rocket in Cameron County, Texas, in 2024.

item.title: A.I. Companies Shatter Fund-Raising Records, as Boom Accelerates
item.description: OpenAI, Anthropic, Waymo and other artificial intelligence companies hauled in $297 billion in funding in the first three months of the year.
item.dc:creator: Erin Griffith

item.title: One of Apple’s First Employees Looks Back at 50 Years
item.description: In 1976, 14-year-old Chris Espinosa rode a moped to his job demonstrating computers made in Steve Jobs’s childhood home. The company has changed, but he’s still there.
item.dc:creator: Kalley Huang
item.media:credit: Ian C. Bates for The New York Times
item.media:description: Chris Espinosa holding his original Apple ID.

item.title: Goodbye ‘Geeky Hunk’? Gmail Users Can Now Change Their Usernames.
item.description: Users who have been saddled with now-cringe email handles since the mid-2000s can now change them without losing any data under a policy Google announced on Wednesday.
item.dc:creator: Sopan Deb
item.media:credit: Sunday Alamba/Associated Press
item.media:description: Google announced on Wednesday that Gmail users would have the option to change their email addresses without losing access to their inboxes.

item.title: Apple’s Fitness Chief, Who Was Accused of Harassment, Is Retiring
item.description: The high-profile executive was a key figure in the company’s expansion into fitness technology and services.
item.dc:creator: Kalley Huang and Tripp Mickle
item.media:credit: Ilya S. Savenok/Getty Images
item.media:description: Apple employees said Jay Blahnik created a toxic work environment.

item.title: WhatsApp Whistle-Blower Suit Is Dismissed
item.description: The former head of security for the messaging app, owned by Meta, had accused the social media company of putting billions of users at risk. A judge said there was not enough evidence supporting claims that he was fired in retaliation.
item.dc:creator: Cecilia Kang
item.media:credit: Christopher Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Attaullah Baig was the head of security for WhatsApp from January 2021 to February 2025.

item.title: Can Science Predict When a Study Won’t Hold Up?
item.description: Conducting research is hard; confirming the results is, too. And artificial intelligence isn’t yet ready to help, a major new study finds.
item.dc:creator: Carl Zimmer
item.media:credit: Kirsten Luce for The New York Times
item.media:description: Brian Nosek, an executive director at the Center for Open Science. In the 2010s, Dr. Nosek and colleagues replicated 100 psychology papers — and matched the original results only 39 percent of the time.

item.title: OpenAI Adds Another $12 Billion to Latest Funding Round
item.description: The start-up has raised a total of $122 billion in a funding round that values the company at $730 billion.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz
item.media:credit: Kylie Cooper/Reuters
item.media:description: Sam Altman, the chief executive of OpenAI. With the deal, OpenAI has cemented its place as one of the most valuable private companies in the world.

item.title: How Instagram’s ‘PG-13’ Branding for Teens Unraveled
item.description: The social media giant, under legal pressure from the Motion Picture Association, has retreated from its use of the movie rating in its marketing.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs and Callie Holtermann
item.media:credit: Loren Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: Instagram introduced Teen Accounts in 2024 in an effort to beef up privacy and address concerns about inappropriate content for teenage users.

item.title: A Cat-and-Mouse Game of Russian Internet Restrictions and Evasion
item.description: As the Kremlin spends heavily on censorship technology, Russians are scrambling to find new ways to circumvent the limits.
item.dc:creator: Valerie Hopkins, Paul Sonne and Oleg Matsnev
item.category: Roskomnadzor (Federal Service for Supervision of Communications, Information Technology and Mass Media) (Russia)
item.media:credit: Anastasia Barashkova/Reuters
item.media:description: With the authorities blocking apps and websites, Russians are trying to find workarounds.

item.title: Whoop, a Wearable Health Device Maker, Raises $575 Million
item.description: With elite athletes like LeBron James and Cristiano Ronaldo as investors, the company, now valued at $10 billion, is courting everyday health enthusiasts.
item.dc:creator: Niko Gallogly
item.media:credit: Whoop
item.media:description: Whoop is one of several companies making wearable technology for wellness enthusiasts.

item.title: What to Know About California’s Executive Order on A.I.
item.description: Gov. Gavin Newsom, a Democrat, issued an order requiring safety and privacy guardrails for artificial intelligence companies contracting with the state.
item.dc:creator: Cecilia Kang
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gov. Gavin Newsom. California has been a leader in tech lawmaking, and was the first state to pass a law mandating safety and transparency from the biggest A.I. companies.

item.title: States Plow Ahead With A.I. Regulation, Defying Trump
item.description: States ranging from California to Utah are taking steps to place guardrails on the technology even after the president ordered them to stop.
item.dc:creator: Cecilia Kang
item.media:credit: Max Whittaker for The New York Times

item.title: From Foe to Ally: The S.E.C. Is Now Writing Crypto-Friendly Policies
item.description: The embrace of crypto by financial regulators under President Trump has entered a new, cozier phase.
item.dc:creator: David Yaffe-Bellany
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times

item.title: Chromebook Remorse: Tech Backlash at Schools Extends Beyond Phones
item.description: No more YouTube or video games on school laptops. Textbooks and pencils are back. Some seventh graders say they prefer learning offline.
item.dc:creator: Natasha Singer and David Robert Elliott

item.title: What Is YouTube’s Dominance Doing to Us? We Asked Its C.E.O.
item.description: Neal Mohan on A.I. slop, parental controls and his platform’s impact on our lives.
item.dc:creator: Lulu Garcia-Navarro
item.media:credit: Devin Oktar Yalkin for The New York Times

item.title: Elon Musk Joins in Phone Call Between Donald Trump and Narendra Modi
item.description: Mr. Musk’s participation was notable because it is rare for a private citizen to be on a call between heads of state. It also suggests that Mr. Musk is back on better terms with the president.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong, Theodore Schleifer, Tyler Pager and Ryan Mac
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Elon Musk and Shivon Zilis arriving for a wedding at Mar-a-Lago last month.

item.title: The Ezra Klein Show: How Fast Will A.I. Agents Rip Through the Economy?
item.description: The Anthropic co-founder Jack Clark tells Ezra Klein what he sees coming in the new era of A.I. agents.
item.dc:creator: Ezra Klein and Rollin Hu
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: An Invisible Bottleneck: A Helium Shortage Threatens the Chip Industry
item.description: With a third of the global supply offline because of the war in Iran, gas companies are scrambling to assure critical A.I. chip makers there will be no disruptions.
item.dc:creator: Meaghan Tobin and Xinyun Wu
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Lam Yik Fei for The New York Times
item.media:description: Air Liquide supplies helium and other gases to the semiconductor industry.

item.title: Why the Verdict Against Meta and YouTube Could Change Social Media
item.description: In a landmark case, a jury found Meta and YouTube guilty of creating products that were addictive. Ryan Mac explains the outcome and what it could mean for tech companies going forward.
item.dc:creator: Ryan Mac, Christina Thornell, Nikolay Nikolov and Edward Vega

item.title: Judge Stays Pentagon’s Labeling of Anthropic as ‘Supply Chain Risk’
item.description: The decision is an early victory for the artificial intelligence company in a rancorous legal battle with the Department of Defense.
item.dc:creator: Mike Isaac
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Marissa Leshnov for The New York Times
item.media:description: The San Francisco office of Anthropic, which won a temporary reprieve in federal court on Thursday.

item.title: Safety Experts Considered LaGuardia Challenging but Not an Outlier
item.description: Regulators, pilots and others in aviation have worried about the kind of runway accident that happened at LaGuardia Airport on Sunday.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi and Karoun Demirjian
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Federal Aviation Administration has been working for years to reduce accidents like the one involving an Air Canada plane and a fire truck at LaGuardia Airport in New York City on Sunday.

item.title: The Clues Binance Missed That Led to Billions in Crypto Flowing to Iran
item.description: The world’s largest crypto exchange is under fire after investigators found accounts moving $1.7 billion to entities linked to Iran. Clues about those accounts were in plain sight for over a year.
item.dc:creator: David Yaffe-Bellany and Michael Forsythe
item.media:credit: Ore Huiying for The New York Times
item.media:description: A villa in Singapore whose address was shared by the chief executive of Blessed Trust, which was a vendor to Binance, and a Chinese entrepreneur with a corporate office that was on a U.S. blacklist.

item.title: U.S. Accuses NewYork-Presbyterian of Reaching Deals That Kept Costs High
item.description: The Justice Department sued the large New York health system, saying it restricted insurance companies from offering patients cheaper options.
item.dc:creator: David McCabe
item.media:credit: Anna Watts for The New York Times
item.media:description: The government said that the NewYork-Presbyterian system had kept health plans from steering patients to less expensive options or creating cheaper plans.

item.title: Shield AI, a Start-Up Making Military Drones, Raises $2 Billion
item.description: The company, which develops autonomous military technology, also plans to buy a maker of simulation software as interest in next-generation defense soars.
item.dc:creator: Michael J. de la Merced
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Caroline Chia/Reuters
item.media:description: Shield AI’s software and drones are being used by several governments, including Ukraine’s.

item.title: Juries Take the Lead in the Push for Child Online Safety
item.description: A pair of verdicts held social media companies accountable for harming young users, highlighting a growing backlash as Congress struggles to pass legislation.
item.dc:creator: Cecilia Kang
item.media:credit: Justin Sullivan/Getty Images
item.media:description: Mark Lanier, a lawyer for a plaintiff who was awarded $6 million in damages against Meta and YouTube, speaking outside Superior Court of Los Angeles County on Wednesday.

item.title: In ‘The AI Doc,’ Sam Altman and Dario Amodei Go on the Record
item.description: “The AI Doc: Or How I Became an Apocaloptimist” tries to cover so much that it ends up being more confusing than clarifying, but parts are fascinating.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson
item.category: The AI Doc: Or How I Became an Apocaloptimist (Movie)
item.media:credit: Focus Features, via Associated Press
item.media:description: A scene from “The AI Doc: Or How I Became an Apocaloptimist,” directed by Daniel Roher and Charlie Tyrell.
